EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper wave dropping south across the Great Lakes and it's associated cold front brought widespread accumulating snow to the region New Year's Eve and into the new year across the ridges of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia. Enhanced orographic lift resulted in higher totals across the Laurel Highlands and eastern West Virginia ridges, with relatively high snow ratios between 18:1 and 21:1. Widespread Advisory level snow was also observed across the region, ranging from 2.5 inches to 5.5 inches outside of the ridges of Pennsylvania and West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall, generally exceeding advisory criteria but falling short of warning criteria, fell across the county with amounts ranging from 3-5 inches and the highest total of 5.8 inches reported by the Public 5 miles west-southwest of Ohiopyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal system brought widespread light to moderate snowfall accumulations to the area. Total snowfall amounts ranged from a light coating to 1 near the coast to 1-4 farther inland. Temperatures near freezing during daytime snowfall fell well below freezing overnight, resulting in slippery road conditions in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 2-4. The highest reported amount was 4.4 in East Stroudsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful and expansive storm system brought widespread heavy wintry precipitation across nearly two thirds of the width of North America, stretching from the southern Rockies and southern Plains, across portions of the Midwest, Deep South, and Southeast, into the Mid-Atlantic and New England. For the Mid-Atlantic region, this was an overrunning precipitation event, with a very cold and dry, arctic airmass in place ahead of the storm. The main low pressure center passed offshore just to the south of the forecast area.||Precipitation began as all snow across the local region during the very early morning hours of January 25th. A transition to sleet occurred from south to north after dawn, reaching as far north as the I-78 to I-80 corridors by the evening before precipitation ended. Some freezing rain occurred across the coastal plain of New Jersey and Delmarva during the afternoon and evening hours. The marine front remained south and east of the I-95 to I-195 corridors. North of the front (along and north of the I-95 to I-195 corridors, including much of the Philadelphia metropolitan area, central and northern Delmarva) into the I-78 corridor, temperatures remained in the 10s (and even single digits near the I-78 corridor) when snow changed to sleet. Total sleet accumulations between 1-3 were reported in this region, on top of approximately 5-10 of snow. North of the I-78 corridor, precipitation remained mostly all snow, where near a foot or more of accumulations were reported, though at least some mixing of sleet was reported as far north as I-80.||The snow and sleet proved very challenging to remove following the storm due to the weight and density of the ice-capped snowpack and below freezing temperatures. At least 3 fatalities related to snow removal occurred in the Lehigh Valley. Snow removal took a few days in some places. Untreated or unplowed pavement remained covered in snow and/or ice for one to two weeks following the storm.||A 17 day period of largely well below freezing temperatures began the day before this storm on January 24th and continued through February 9th. This was one of the coldest 17 day periods in several decades for much of the region at the time. Mean temperatures in the period ranged from the mid 10s to low 20s. The extreme cold preserved the ice-capped, glacier-like snowpack for approximately 3 to 4 weeks following the storm. The snowpack remained across the entire region until about the third week of February, before melting away completely in most areas due to light rainfall and low temperatures remaining above freezing for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow and sleet accumulation totals mainly ranged from 13-17. The highest report received was 18.1 in East Stroudsburg.

The snowpack remained across the entire region until about the third week of February, before melting away completely in most areas due to light rainfall and low temperatures remaining above freezing for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow and sleet accumulation totals mainly ranged from 8-12. The highest report received was 12.5 in Malvern.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful and expansive storm system brought widespread heavy wintry precipitation across nearly two thirds of the width of North America, stretching from the southern Rockies and southern Plains, across portions of the Midwest, Deep South, and Southeast, into the Mid-Atlantic and New England. For the Mid-Atlantic region, this was an overrunning precipitation event, with a very cold and dry, arctic airmass in place ahead of the storm. The main low pressure center passed offshore just to the south of the forecast area.||Precipitation began as all snow across the local region during the very early morning hours of January 25th. A transition to sleet occurred from south to north after dawn, reaching as far north as the I-78 to I-80 corridors by the evening before precipitation ended. Some freezing rain occurred across the coastal plain of New Jersey and Delmarva during the afternoon and evening hours. The marine front remained south and east of the I-95 to I-195 corridors. North of the front (along and north of the I-95 to I-195 corridors, including much of the Philadelphia metropolitan area, central and northern Delmarva) into the I-78 corridor, temperatures remained in the 10s (and even single digits near the I-78 corridor) when snow changed to sleet. Total sleet accumulations between 1-3 were reported in this region, on top of approximately 5-10 of snow. North of the I-78 corridor, precipitation remained mostly all snow, where near a foot or more of accumulations were reported, though at least some mixing of sleet was reported as far north as I-80.||The snow and sleet proved very challenging to remove following the storm due to the weight and density of the ice-capped snowpack and below freezing temperatures. At least 3 fatalities related to snow removal occurred in the Lehigh Valley. Snow removal took a few days in some places. Untreated or unplowed pavement remained covered in snow and/or ice for one to two weeks following the storm.||A 17 day period of largely well below freezing temperatures began the day before this storm on January 24th and continued through February 9th. This was one of the coldest 17 day periods in several decades for much of the region at the time. Mean temperatures in the period ranged from the mid 10s to low 20s. The extreme cold preserved the ice-capped, glacier-like snowpack for approximately 3 to 4 weeks following the storm. The snowpack remained across the entire region until about the third week of February, before melting away completely in most areas due to light rainfall and low temperatures remaining above freezing for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow and sleet accumulation totals mainly ranged from 7-9. The highest report received was 9.3 at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light rainfall occurred across portions of the region. Temperatures were near freezing across the Pocono Plateau, resulting in some areas of freezing rain at higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain resulted in some areas of icing on elevated surfaces at higher elevations of the Pocono Plateau. The Pocono Mountains Municipal Airport reported 0.12 of ice accretion. A nearby CoCoRaHS observer reported 0.12 of liquid, with a brief period of icing of elevated surfaces during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front dove through the area from north to south ushering in strong cold advection in its wake. A narrow, strong line of convective snow developed along the boundary sinking across the entire county warning area. There were numerous reports of visibility of a quarter mile or less, wind gusts greater than 30 mph, and rapid accumulation on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PennDOT sensor I-70 Eastbound at Mile Marker 48 measured less than a quarter mile visibility in a snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convective snow showers developed along an early morning cold front sweeping west to east across the region. The strongest convergence along the front and mid-level forcing was north of Pittsburgh where impactful snow squalls resulted in visibility reduction to a quarter mile or less and a quick accumulation of snow on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS KFKL measured quarter mile visibility in a snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of two cold front passages resulted in convective snow shower development in the afternoon hours. Convergence along the boundary was strongest north of Pittsburgh coincident with where impactful snow squalls developed. Numerous reports from ASOS and Department of Highways sensors measured visibility of a quarter mile or less with quick accumulation on surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PennDOT sensor I-80 at I-376 measured less than quarter mile visibility in a snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing through Ontario dragged a strong cold front west to east across the region in the afternoon hours. Boundary orthogonal bands of snow squalls developed ahead of the front with steep low level lapse rates and non-zero surface-based CAPE. The result was a widespread area of snow bands reducing visibility to a quarter mile or less with gusty wind greater than 35 mph and rapid accumulation on roadways resulting in at least one highway accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of less than quarter mile visibility in a snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Arctic Front across southern Ontario and western New York began to shift southeasterly during the late evening hours of December 31, 2025 and continued into the early morning hours of January 1, 2026. This potent cold front allowed for a line of snow squalls to form across all of central Pennsylvania, with widespread whiteout conditions are the line of squalls moved through the area. Whiteout conditions were observed at the NWS State College office, along with by multiple spotters in Centre, Mifflin, Blair, and York counties. No accidents or injuries were reported during these snow squalls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall brought visibility less than one quarter of a mile with heavy snow observed on US Route 15 south of Blossburg. Heavy snowfall allowed for quick accumulation on the roadway in a span of 10 to 15 minutes. Winds were observed to gust near 25 mph as the snow squall moved through the location during the early morning hours of January 1, 2026.

Whiteout conditions were observed at the NWS State College office, along with by multiple spotters in Centre, Mifflin, Blair, and York counties. No accidents or injuries were reported during these snow squalls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall was observed at Clearfield Lawrence Airport, with visibility at or below one quarter of a mile for the duration of the snow squall with heavy snow being reported at the airfield in the early morning hours of January 1, 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow behind a departing area of low pressure allowed for lake effect snowfall across portions of northwestern Pennsylvania beginning on December 29, 2025 and continuing through January 1, 2026. Ample moisture in place from Lake Erie and continued northwesterly flow allowed for a long-duration, but light snowfall between December 29th and December 31st before heavier lake effect snow ramped up on January 1st across Warren and McKean counties. As a potent cold front moved across the area on December 31st and into January 1st, winds began to shift westerly, allowing for heavy upslope snow showers across Somerset County to allow for snowfall beginning December 31st and into January 1st. No impacts were reported with the heavy lake effect and upslope snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began across Somerset County during the afternoon hours of December 31st and continued into the evening hours of January 1st. Light snow was mainly observed from December on December 31st, with two to three inches of snowfall. Upslope snowfall across the western ridges of Somerset County became heavier on January 1st, allowing for storm-total snowfall accumulations between 8 and 9 inches, with other portions of the county approaching 5 to 6 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions across much of central Pennsylvania persisted throughout the late fall and early winter months, allowing for severe drought to form across portions of southern and eastern Pennsylvania in January 2026. Multiple counties across central Pennsylvania were put under a Drought Watch by the Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection, with Fulton County being put under a Drought Warning. Multiple instances of voluntary water reductions were put throughout central Pennsylvania, with two boroughs and a township in Schuylkill County put under a mandatory water conservation notice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 (severe) drought conditions began across Adams County on January 13, 2026 with dry conditions continuing through the end of January 2026. The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection issued a Drought Watch for Adams County on January 8th. Voluntary water consumption reduction by 10% to 15% was urged due to the Drought Watch declaration by Pennsylvania American Water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of strong low pressure tracked up the eastern seaboard downstream of a digging positive to neutrally tilted trough. Deep moisture through the dendritic growth zone and strong forcing resulted in widespread significant snowfall accumulation greater than 6 across the entire county warning area with amounts generally falling between 10-18. Localized amounts under narrow frontogenetically-forced bands reached up to 22 inches. An intruding mid-level warm nose reduced snow amounts slightly in northern West Virginia and resulted in a period of some mixing of snow with freezing rain and sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow accumulation generally ranging from 12 to 16 inches was reported across the county with the highest total of 20 inches reported by the public 1 mile west of West Mayfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Arctic Front across southern Ontario and western New York began to shift southeasterly during the late evening hours of December 31, 2025 and continued into the early morning hours of January 1, 2026. This potent cold front allowed for a line of snow squalls to form across all of central Pennsylvania, with widespread whiteout conditions are the line of squalls moved through the area. Whiteout conditions were observed at the NWS State College office, along with by multiple spotters in Centre, Mifflin, Blair, and York counties. No accidents or injuries were reported during these snow squalls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall brought visibility less than one quarter of a mile with heavy snow observed on Interstate 80 near Kylertown. Heavy snowfall allowed for quick accumulation on the roadway in a span of 10 to 15 minutes. Winds were observed to gust near 30 mph as the snow squall moved through the location during the early morning hours of January 1, 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions across much of central Pennsylvania persisted throughout the late fall and early winter months, allowing for severe drought to form across portions of southern and eastern Pennsylvania in January 2026. Multiple counties across central Pennsylvania were put under a Drought Watch by the Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection, with Fulton County being put under a Drought Warning. Multiple instances of voluntary water reductions were put throughout central Pennsylvania, with two boroughs and a township in Schuylkill County put under a mandatory water conservation notice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite some light wind overnight, low dew points and clear skies provided opportunity for sheltered areas to radiate efficiently overnight and minimum wind chill values on a very localized scale to reach colder than -20F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow in the wake of a low pressure system stationed over the northern Atlantic allowed for snow showers to generate during the morning hours of January 19, 2026. Subsidence ahead of a surface trough allowed for some clearing ahead of the snow showers, allowing for meager instability in an environment with ample lift to allow for snow squalls for form during the early afternoon hours. Multiple snow squalls were observed across the western half of Pennsylvania, allowing for near-zero visibility and snowfall accumulating on roadways in these squalls. No injuries or accidents were reported during these squalls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. The ASOS at the Bradford Regional Airport reported visibility below one quarter of a mile in heavy snow as a snow squall moved through. Wind gusts of 30 mph were observed with the squall.

This potent cold front allowed for a line of snow squalls to form across all of central Pennsylvania, with widespread whiteout conditions are the line of squalls moved through the area. Whiteout conditions were observed at the NWS State College office, along with by multiple spotters in Centre, Mifflin, Blair, and York counties. No accidents or injuries were reported during these snow squalls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall was observed at Penn Valley Airport in Selinsgrove, with visibility at or below one quarter of a mile for the duration of the snow squall with heavy snow being reported at the airfield in the early morning hours of January 1, 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow in the wake of a low pressure system stationed over the northern Atlantic allowed for snow showers to generate during the morning hours of January 19, 2026. Subsidence ahead of a surface trough allowed for some clearing ahead of the snow showers, allowing for meager instability in an environment with ample lift to allow for snow squalls for form during the early afternoon hours. Multiple snow squalls were observed across the western half of Pennsylvania, allowing for near-zero visibility and snowfall accumulating on roadways in these squalls. No injuries or accidents were reported during these squalls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. The PennDOT RWIS station on State Route 219 at State Route 6 near Lantz Corners reported visibility below one quarter of a mile with snow accumulating on the roadway as a snow squall moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air behind a cold frontal passage allowed for temperatures to plummet rapidly during the evening hours of January 23, 2026 and into the mid-morning hours of January 24th. The cold front began to move eastward overnight; however, stalled out across portions of central Pennsylvania, allowing for the coldest temperatures to remain across western Pennsylvania. A combination of the colder temperatures behind the cold front and gusty winds along and behind the front allowed for wind chills to dip below -20 degrees across portions of western Pennsylvania by the early morning hours of January 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold temperatures coupled with gusty winds allowed for dangerously cold wind chills in Cambria County during the late evening hours of January 23rd. These dangerously cold wind chill continued into the morning hours of January 24, 2026. The coldest wind chills were observed near Johnstown, where John Murtha Cambria County Airport registered a -26 degree wind chill during the late evening hours of January 23rd before winds began to become more light.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air in the wake of a winter storm that brought widespread snowfall to central Pennsylvania allowed for many locations to experience a prolonged period of cold weather beginning on January 27th and continuing into January 31, 2026. Cold air in the wake of the winter system allowed for a period of much cooler than average temperatures with gusty winds also allowing for dangerously cold wind chills across western Pennsylvania January 26th and into January 27th. High pressure in the wake of an Arctic Front passage on January 30th allowed for bitterly cold temperatures to continue into January 31st, especially across western Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme cold impacted Cambria County on the morning of January 27. The coldest observed wind chill was -24 degrees at the John Murtha Johnstown Cambria County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary cold front from an associated upper-level low pressure system tracking from the Ohio Valley allowed for snow shower development during the morning hours of January 11th. Clear skies with cold advection aloft allowed for meager, but sufficient, instability across the western half of Pennsylvania in an environment of ample lift and moisture to produce snow squalls. These snow squalls produced multiple instances of visibility below one-quarter of a mile, with multiple accidents with one reported on US Route 6 in McKean County and another accident on US Route 322 near Port Matilda. No injuries were reported from these accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall moved across Cambria County on the morning of January 11, 2026. The ASOS at the John Murtha Johnstown Cambria County Airport reported a visibility drop to one quarter of a mile in heavy snow as the squall moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong 1033 mb high pressure nosing ridging in from the west allowed for efficient radiational cooling and minimum wind chill values ranging from -5 to -15F and as cold as -22F at KDUJ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill values generally ranged from -5 to -15 degrees across the county with the exception of the Dubois ASOS which recorded -22 degrees in the morning of the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong 1042 mb high pressure centered just to the northwest of the area provided plenty of subsidence and optimal conditions for cold temperatures overnight with wind chills dropping into the negative single digits. KDUJ recorded a -23F degree wind chill in the very early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chill values ranged from -10 to -20 across most of the county with the exception of the Dubois ASOS which recorded a -23 degree wind chill in the very early morning hours of the 24th.

These snow squalls produced multiple instances of visibility below one-quarter of a mile, with multiple accidents with one reported on US Route 6 in McKean County and another accident on US Route 322 near Port Matilda. No injuries were reported from these accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. The ASOS at the Clearfield Lawrence Township Airport reported visibility of one quarter mile in heavy snow as a snow squall moved through. Wind gusts of 25 mph were also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Arctic Front across southern Ontario and western New York began to shift southeasterly during the late evening hours of December 31, 2025 and continued into the early morning hours of January 1, 2026. This potent cold front allowed for a line of snow squalls to form across all of central Pennsylvania, with widespread whiteout conditions are the line of squalls moved through the area. Whiteout conditions were observed at the NWS State College office, along with by multiple spotters in Centre, Mifflin, Blair, and York counties. No accidents or injuries were reported during these snow squalls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall brought visibility less than one quarter of a mile with heavy snow observed on Interstate 80 near Penfield. Heavy snowfall allowed for quick accumulation on the roadway in a span of 10 to 15 minutes. Winds were observed to gust near 20 to 25 mph as the snow squall moved through the location during the early morning hours of January 1, 2026.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall brought visibility less than one quarter of a mile with heavy snow observed across eastern Tioga County. This heavy snow covered US Route 6. Winds were observed to gust near 25 mph as the snow squall moved through the location during the early morning of January 1, 2026.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow behind a departing area of low pressure allowed for lake effect snowfall across portions of northwestern Pennsylvania beginning on December 29, 2025 and continuing through January 1, 2026. Ample moisture in place from Lake Erie and continued northwesterly flow allowed for a long-duration, but light snowfall between December 29th and December 31st before heavier lake effect snow ramped up on January 1st across Warren and McKean counties. As a potent cold front moved across the area on December 31st and into January 1st, winds began to shift westerly, allowing for heavy upslope snow showers across Somerset County to allow for snowfall beginning December 31st and into January 1st. No impacts were reported with the heavy lake effect and upslope snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow began during the morning hours of December 29th and continued through the afternoon hours of January 1st. Light snow was mainly observed from December 29th through December 31st, with two to three inches of snowfall. Heavy snow began during the early morning hours of January 1st, mainly confined to the northern tier of Warren County, where 7.6 inches of snowfall was observed west of Russell by the end of the snowfall event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of strong low pressure tracked up the eastern seaboard downstream of a digging positive to neutrally tilted trough. Deep moisture through the dendritic growth zone and strong forcing resulted in widespread significant snowfall accumulation greater than 6 across the entire county warning area with amounts generally falling between 10-18. Localized amounts under narrow frontogenetically-forced bands reached up to 22 inches. An intruding mid-level warm nose reduced snow amounts slightly in northern West Virginia and resulted in a period of some mixing of snow with freezing rain and sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow accumulation generally ranging from 7 to 14 inches was reported across the county with the highest total of 14.3 inches reported by a CoCoRaHS observer 2 miles south-southwest of Wexford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow accumulation generally ranging from 9 to 13 inches with the highest total of 16 inches from the public in Dayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough stationed over western Pennsylvania during the early morning hours with ample moisture and lift allowed for snowfall to begin across much of western Pennsylvania during the late evening hours of January 24th and the early morning hours of January 25, 2026. Moderate-to-heavy snowfall rates were observed at onset with low-level frontogenesis allowing for a moderate snowfall band across portions of western Pennsylvania prior to sunrise. Ample moisture in place with an approaching area of low pressure from the Lower Ohio River Valley also allowed for snowfall to begin across portions of southeastern Pennsylvania ahead of sunrise on January 25th. The progressive movement of the aforementioned trough and low pressure system allowed for a shorter duration of snowfall than could have occurred, with snowfall gradually tapering off across much of the area by the evening hours of January 26th. Westerly flow in the wake of the system did allow for continued upslope snow showers across the western highlands, which continued to allow for additional accumulations. Much of central Pennsylvania observed heavy snowfall, with Harrisburg International Airport and Williamsport Regional Airport breaking daily snowfall records, which have been tracked since 1888 and 1895, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began in the early morning of January 25 and continued into the evening, with most of Northumberland seeing 8-10 inches of snow. The highest snowfall total in the county occurred near Sunbury, where 13.5 inches was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough couple with an area of low pressure north of the region allowed for ample lift and moisture to funnel into northern Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours of January 17th. Afternoon clearing ahead of snow showers allowed for marginal instability to form, allowing for a couple of snow squalls to form across Warren and McKean Counties. As the squalls moved farther east, limited instability allowed for the snow squalls to weaken with scattered snow showers continuing across northern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A snow squall was observed at Bradford Regional Airport, with visibility at or below one quarter of a mile for the duration of the snow squall with heavy snow being reported at the airfield in the early evening hours of January 17, 2026. This snow squall was also observed further south in Lantz Corner with webcams indicating snow accumulating on the roadways with heavy snow and visibility below one quarter of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough couple with an area of low pressure north of the region allowed for ample lift and moisture to funnel into northern Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours of January 17th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. Webcams in Warren outlined near-zero visibility with heavy snowfall being observed on the webcam. Roads rapidly became snow covered over the course of 5 to 10 minutes within the snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of strong low pressure tracked up the eastern seaboard downstream of a digging positive to neutrally tilted trough. Deep moisture through the dendritic growth zone and strong forcing resulted in widespread significant snowfall accumulation greater than 6 across the entire county warning area with amounts generally falling between 10-18. Localized amounts under narrow frontogenetically-forced bands reached up to 22 inches. An intruding mid-level warm nose reduced snow amounts slightly in northern West Virginia and resulted in a period of some mixing of snow with freezing rain and sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow accumulation generally ranging from 7 to 10 inches was reported across the county with the highest total of 10.5 inches reported by the public 4 miles northwest of Ohiopyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful and expansive storm system brought widespread heavy wintry precipitation across nearly two thirds of the width of North America, stretching from the southern Rockies and southern Plains, across portions of the Midwest, Deep South, and Southeast, into the Mid-Atlantic and New England. For the Mid-Atlantic region, this was an overrunning precipitation event, with a very cold and dry, arctic airmass in place ahead of the storm. The main low pressure center passed offshore just to the south of the forecast area.||Precipitation began as all snow across the local region during the very early morning hours of January 25th. A transition to sleet occurred from south to north after dawn, reaching as far north as the I-78 to I-80 corridors by the evening before precipitation ended. Some freezing rain occurred across the coastal plain of New Jersey and Delmarva during the afternoon and evening hours. The marine front remained south and east of the I-95 to I-195 corridors. North of the front (along and north of the I-95 to I-195 corridors, including much of the Philadelphia metropolitan area, central and northern Delmarva) into the I-78 corridor, temperatures remained in the 10s (and even single digits near the I-78 corridor) when snow changed to sleet. Total sleet accumulations between 1-3 were reported in this region, on top of approximately 5-10 of snow. North of the I-78 corridor, precipitation remained mostly all snow, where near a foot or more of accumulations were reported, though at least some mixing of sleet was reported as far north as I-80.||The snow and sleet proved very challenging to remove following the storm due to the weight and density of the ice-capped snowpack and below freezing temperatures. At least 3 fatalities related to snow removal occurred in the Lehigh Valley. Snow removal took a few days in some places. Untreated or unplowed pavement remained covered in snow and/or ice for one to two weeks following the storm.||A 17 day period of largely well below freezing temperatures began the day before this storm on January 24th and continued through February 9th. This was one of the coldest 17 day periods in several decades for much of the region at the time. Mean temperatures in the period ranged from the mid 10s to low 20s. The extreme cold preserved the ice-capped, glacier-like snowpack for approximately 3 to 4 weeks following the storm. The snowpack remained across the entire region until about the third week of February, before melting away completely in most areas due to light rainfall and low temperatures remaining above freezing for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow and sleet accumulation totals mainly ranged from 12-15. The highest report received was 16.7 in Lower Towamensing Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Icy roads were blamed for a head-on collision that occurred just before 8 AM EST on State Route 11 about a half-mile north of College Avenue in Nicholson. A pick up truck lost control and veered into oncoming traffic. Unfortunately, the driver in the other vehicle passed away at the scene as a result of the crash. Temperatures had dropped just below freezing shortly before the time of the crash in the wake of rain showers and a cold frontal boundary that moved through, resulting in the development of icy conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy road conditions on State Route 11 led to a pick up truck losing control and colliding with another vehicle head on. The female driver of the other vehicle unfortunately passed away at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 19-year-old man lost control of a pickup truck just after 6:00 AM EST when temperatures dropped below freezing in the wake of rain showers and a cold front that moved through. This resulted in icy road conditions developing on State Route 92 in Gibson Township, PA about a half-mile south of State Route 2071. The pickup truck slid into an embankment, striking several trees before rolling over. The young man was partially ejected from the vehicle and unfortunately passed away at the scene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 19-year-old man lost control of a pickup truck when temperatures dropped below freezing in the wake of rain showers that moved through. Icy road conditions developed on State Route 92 in Gibson Township, PA about a half-mile south of State Route 2071 and the truck slid into an embankment and struck several trees. The young man unfortunately passed away at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough stationed over western Pennsylvania during the early morning hours with ample moisture and lift allowed for snowfall to begin across much of western Pennsylvania during the late evening hours of January 24th and the early morning hours of January 25, 2026. Moderate-to-heavy snowfall rates were observed at onset with low-level frontogenesis allowing for a moderate snowfall band across portions of western Pennsylvania prior to sunrise. Ample moisture in place with an approaching area of low pressure from the Lower Ohio River Valley also allowed for snowfall to begin across portions of southeastern Pennsylvania ahead of sunrise on January 25th. The progressive movement of the aforementioned trough and low pressure system allowed for a shorter duration of snowfall than could have occurred, with snowfall gradually tapering off across much of the area by the evening hours of January 26th. Westerly flow in the wake of the system did allow for continued upslope snow showers across the western highlands, which continued to allow for additional accumulations. Much of central Pennsylvania observed heavy snowfall, with Harrisburg International Airport and Williamsport Regional Airport breaking daily snowfall records, which have been tracked since 1888 and 1895, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began in the evening of January 24 and continued into the evening of January 25, with most of Franklin County seeing 6-8 inches of snow and sleet. The highest snowfall total in the county occurred near Chambersburg, where 9 inches was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked east of the Ohio Valley before reforming off the Northeast coast on Sunday the 25th. Snow spread in from south to north during the morning hours, becoming heaviest from mid-morning into the early evening. The snow gradually tapered off early Monday morning, with some light snow showers and flurries lingering across northeast PA. Strong, gusty winds persisted through Monday afternoon, creating localized blowing snow and reduced visibility in parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 10 and 17 inches.
